title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
description: Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder where preorder is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and inorder is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.
constraints: preorder and inorder consist of unique values
Each value of inorder also appears in preorder
preorder is guaranteed to be the preorder traversal of the tree
inorder is guaranteed to be the inorder traversal of the tree
thinking_questions: Preorder: root is always first. How does that help you find the root?
Once you know the root, how does the inorder array tell you which nodes are in the left vs right subtree?
After splitting, what do you recurse on?

hints.L1: Preorder's first element is always the root. Find that root in inorder to determine the left/right subtree sizes.
hints.L2: Root = preorder[0]. Find its index in inorder. Left subtree has inorder[:idx] elements, right has inorder[idx+1:]. Recurse on corresponding preorder slices.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: preorder[0]=root; inorder split defines left/right subtrees
if not preorder or not inorder: return None
root_val = preorder[0]
root = TreeNode(root_val)
mid = inorder.index(root_val)
root.left = buildTree(preorder[1:1+mid], inorder[:mid])
root.right = buildTree(preorder[1+mid:], inorder[mid+1:])
return root
# Time: O(n²) due to list slicing and .index()
# Optimize to O(n): HashMap for inorder index lookup + pass indices instead of slicing

follow_up_questions: How do you optimize from O(n²) to O(n) using a HashMap?
What if you had postorder and inorder instead? How does the approach change?
Why can you NOT construct a unique tree from preorder alone?
solution_notes: preorder[0]=root; find in inorder for left/right split; recurse. O(n²) naive, O(n) with HashMap for inorder indices.
